title: Git Fundamentals
objectives: Understand Git via a real-life analogy
Know the 3 areas (working dir, staging, repo) and the daily commands
Understand branches, merges, and rebases without confusion
Recover from mistakes safely (reflog, revert, reset)

sections.markdown:
**Imagine writing a novel with your friends across a shared Google Doc.**

- **Your local copy** is the manuscript on your desk. You can scribble freely.
- The **shared Doc** is the central version everyone builds on.
- Every time you finish a chapter, you **save a snapshot** with a note — 'Chapter 3: introduced villain.' Every snapshot is a **commit**.
- If your friend and you edited the same paragraph, you have to **merge** — decide which version wins.
- If you regret a chapter, you can **jump back** to any snapshot.

**Git is that but disciplined**: every save is deliberate, every change traceable to a person, every mistake reversible. It's the tool humanity built for versioning source code — and it turned out to be the tool for versioning anything.

**Git** is a distributed version-control system that tracks changes as a graph of immutable **commits** — snapshots of your project at a moment in time. It lets many developers work on the same code without stepping on each other, and lets anyone see, compare, or revert any moment in the project's history.
## The 3 areas — the mental model everyone needs

sections.code:
┌─────────────────┐  git add    ┌──────────────┐  git commit   ┌──────────────┐
  │  Working dir    │ ──────────► │   Staging    │ ─────────────► │  Repository  │
  │  (your edits)   │             │   (index)    │                │  (history)    │
  └─────────────────┘             └──────────────┘                └──────────────┘
         ▲                                                                │
         │                          git checkout                          │
         └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Working directory: files you can edit right now.
  Staging area:      the changes you're preparing to commit.
  Repository:        the permanent history of commits (in `.git/`).

  Rule of thumb:
     git add  = 'I want THIS change in the next commit.'
     git commit = 'take a snapshot with a message.'
     git push = 'send my snapshots to the shared server (GitHub/GitLab).'

sections.markdown: ## The 10 commands that cover 95% of daily use

rows: Stage changes for the next commit
Send your commits to the remote
Create + switch to a new branch

sections.markdown:
## Branches — how many developers work in parallel

A **branch** is a pointer to a commit. When you start a new feature, you create a branch — your work doesn't touch the main line until you merge it in.

The common convention:

sections.code:
main                    ○──○──○──────○──○──── (production; protected)
                              │           │
                              │           │ merge
  feat/auth-flow              └○──○──○───┘        (developer's feature branch)

  1. git switch -c feat/auth-flow    (create branch off main)
  2. write code, commit, push
  3. open a Pull Request on GitHub
  4. teammate reviews, requests changes, approves
  5. merge into main → CI/CD deploys

sections.markdown: ## Merge vs Rebase — the daily choice

rows: Combines branches with a merge commit
Replays your commits on top of the target branch
Only on YOUR branch (never on shared main)
Clean up local commits before pushing

sections.markdown: **The GOLDEN RULE of rebase:** *never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch.* You'd rewrite history teammates were building on, forcing them into merge hell. Rebase your own local commits — never their commits or shared history.

## A complete sample program — a realistic feature flow

Here's what your day looks like using Git for a real feature:

sections.code:
# --- Monday: Start a new feature ---
git switch main
git pull                                    # get the latest
git switch -c feat/login-form               # new branch

# ... edit files, run tests locally ...

git add lib/features/auth/login_screen.dart
git status                                  # see what's staged
git commit -m 'feat(auth): add login screen skeleton'
git push -u origin feat/login-form          # push, set upstream


# --- Tuesday: You realize your last commit was bad ---
# Option A: Undo the last commit but KEEP the changes staged (most common)
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Option B: Undo the last commit AND discard the changes (careful!)
# git reset --hard HEAD~1

# Now edit more, re-commit
git commit -am 'feat(auth): add login screen with validation'


# --- Wednesday: Sync with main before your PR ---
git switch main
git pull
git switch feat/login-form
git rebase main                             # replay your work on top of latest main
# (resolve any conflicts, then git rebase --continue)
git push --force-with-lease                 # safe force-push YOUR branch


# --- Thursday: Open PR, get review, merge on GitHub ---
# (usually via GitHub UI — 'Squash and merge')


# --- Friday: Delete the branch (locally + remote) ---
git switch main
git pull
git branch -d feat/login-form               # local
git push origin --delete feat/login-form    # remote


# --- OOPS: I made a bad reset yesterday and lost work! ---
git reflog                                  # shows every HEAD move — your safety net
# find the SHA you want; then:
git checkout <sha>                          # look at it
git reset --hard <sha>                      # restore (if you're sure)

sections.markdown: **`git reflog` is your safety net.** Almost nothing in git is truly lost for the first 30 days — reflog remembers every HEAD position. If you accidentally reset, force-push, or delete a branch, `git reflog` finds the SHA you can restore. Every git user should know this exists.
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Committing everything at once with a 'wip' message.**

Good commits are focused (one logical change) and messaged like a mini email — subject line describing WHY. `git commit -m 'wip'` tells future you nothing when debugging in six months.

**Mistake 2: Force-pushing to a shared branch.**

Rewrites history teammates were building on. If you must fix a public branch, use `git revert` (creates a new commit undoing changes) — not reset + force-push.

**Mistake 3: Committing secrets or huge files.**

API keys, `.env`, node_modules, build artifacts — none of these belong in git. Use `.gitignore`. Once a secret is committed and pushed, it's *forever* in history unless you rewrite (painful). Rotate the key instead.
## Best practices
**1. Small, focused commits with imperative subject lines.** `add login form` not `added login form and fixed some bugs and also did the css`.
**2. Never commit to main directly on team projects.** Always branches + PRs + review. Enforce with GitHub branch protection.
**3. Learn `git rebase -i` for cleaning up your own commits.** Before opening a PR, squash the 'wip', 'fix', 'oops' commits into meaningful ones.
**4. Use `git stash` when you need to switch branches with uncommitted work.** `stash` = 'set this aside for now', `stash pop` = 'put it back'.
**5. Configure `.gitignore` from day one.** For Flutter: `build/`, `.dart_tool/`, `.idea/`, `*.iml`, `pubspec.lock` (arguable). GitHub provides templates.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Full round trip.** Init a repo. Add a file. Commit. Branch. Change. Commit. Merge back. Delete the branch.

**Why:** Build muscle memory.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Resolve a merge conflict.** Create two branches that edit the same line. Merge one, then try to merge the other. See the conflict markers. Resolve. Commit.

**Why:** Everyone hits conflicts; being calm about them is a real skill.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Interactive rebase to clean up.** Make 5 messy commits (`wip`, `fix typo`, `wip 2`, etc.). Use `git rebase -i HEAD~5` to squash and reword into 1-2 meaningful commits.

**Why:** This is how you polish PRs before review.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — `git bisect` to find a bug.** Have a working commit and a broken commit far apart. Use `git bisect start` / `bad` / `good` to binary-search which commit introduced the bug.

**Why:** For hard-to-reproduce regressions, bisect is a superpower.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Explain Git's three areas.**

**A:** Git tracks changes through three areas. The **working directory** is your files as you're editing them — everything is fair game. The **staging area** (or 'index') is where you place specific changes you want to include in the next commit — `git add` moves changes from working to staging. The **repository** is the permanent history of commits, stored under `.git/` — `git commit` snapshots the staged changes as a commit. The reason for three areas: you can craft a focused commit even when your working directory has lots of unrelated changes. `git add -p` (interactive) lets you stage specific hunks. This staging step is what separates Git from simpler tools like RCS or SVN.

**Q2: Merge vs rebase — when do you use each?**

**A:** **Merge** combines two branches with a merge commit that has two parents — preserving the branch structure in history. Safe to use on shared branches because it doesn't rewrite anything. **Rebase** replays your commits on top of another branch, creating brand-new commits (different SHAs). This gives a clean, linear history — no merge commits — but it rewrites history, so it's dangerous if others were building on your original commits. The golden rule: **rebase your own local branches to keep them clean; merge for combining shared branches**. Never rebase commits you've already pushed to a shared branch.

**Q3: How do you undo the last commit (both keeping and discarding changes)?**

**A:** Three flavors of `git reset`:

- **Keep changes in the working directory and staging**: `git reset --soft HEAD~1`. The commit is gone; all its changes are still staged. Great when the commit was fine but you want to add more.
- **Keep changes only in the working directory**: `git reset HEAD~1` (or `--mixed`, the default). The commit is gone, changes are unstaged.
- **Discard the changes entirely**: `git reset --hard HEAD~1`. Deletes the commit AND all its changes. **Use with care** — this is the one people regret. `git reflog` can recover if you act within 30 days.

If the commit was already pushed to a shared branch, don't reset — use `git revert HEAD` to create a new commit that undoes it, preserving history.

**Q4: What's the difference between `git revert` and `git reset`?**

**A:** **`git reset`** rewrites history — it moves the branch pointer backward and (depending on `--soft/--mixed/--hard`) may erase commits. Safe for private local branches; catastrophic on shared branches because it rewrites history teammates depend on. **`git revert`** creates a *new commit* that undoes the changes of a previous commit — history is *preserved and extended*. Safe on shared branches because nothing is rewritten. If you find a bad commit in main that others already pulled: `git revert <sha>` and push. If you regret a commit you just made locally and haven't pushed: `git reset` is fine. The distinction: reset erases, revert appends-an-undo.

**Q5: How do you recover a deleted branch or a lost commit?**

**A:** Use `git reflog`. Unlike `git log` which shows the current branch's history, **reflog shows every position HEAD has ever been** in your local repo — every checkout, commit, reset, merge, rebase. Almost nothing is truly lost for 30 days (the default reflog expiry). If you accidentally deleted a branch, `git reflog` will show a commit like `abc123 HEAD@{5}: checkout: moving from feat/x to main` — the SHA `abc123` is still valid; `git branch feat/x abc123` restores it. If you did a bad `reset --hard`, reflog shows the SHA before the reset — `git reset --hard <sha>` restores. **Reflog is your safety net; every Git user should know it exists.**

**Q6: What does `git cherry-pick` do, and when would you use it?**

**A:** `git cherry-pick <sha>` takes an existing commit from anywhere and applies it as a new commit on your current branch. The most common use case: a hotfix. Say a critical bug was fixed on `main`, and you also need the fix on the `release-1.2` branch. Instead of merging all of main into release (which would bring unrelated changes), you cherry-pick just that one fix commit. Another use: pulling one commit from a colleague's branch without merging their whole branch. Downside: cherry-pick creates a new commit with a new SHA, so the same 'logical' change now exists at two SHAs — merges between the branches later can get confusing. Use sparingly, primarily for hotfixes.

**Q7: What is `.gitignore` and what belongs there?**

**A:** `.gitignore` is a file at the repo root listing patterns of files Git should NOT track. Anything generated or environment-specific belongs there: **build artifacts** (`build/`, `dist/`, `target/`), **dependency directories** (`node_modules/`, `.dart_tool/`), **IDE settings** (`.idea/`, `.vscode/`), **OS files** (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`), and — most importantly — **secrets and env-specific config** (`.env`, credentials JSON files, API keys). Never commit secrets. Once pushed, they're in history forever unless you rewrite (`git filter-repo`) — practically, rotate the secret and move on. GitHub provides `.gitignore` templates for every language framework. Add one to every new project on day one.

## Real-world examples

- **Every open-source project on GitHub** uses this exact flow: fork → branch → PR → review → merge. If you can navigate this, you can contribute to any of them.
- **Trunk-based development** (Google, Facebook, Netflix) — everyone commits to main behind feature flags; branches are short-lived (hours to days).
- **Git-Flow** (older enterprise convention) — main, develop, feature/*, release/*, hotfix/* branches. Heavy but explicit. Being replaced by trunk-based in most modern teams.
- **Deploy-from-main** — every merge to main triggers CI/CD to production. Common in high-velocity teams; requires disciplined tests and staged rollouts.

## Advanced notes

- **`git bisect`** — automated binary search through history to find the commit that introduced a bug. Genuinely magical for hard-to-find regressions.
- **`git rebase --interactive`** (`-i`) — squash, reorder, edit, drop commits before pushing. Essential for polishing PRs.
- **`git worktree`** — check out multiple branches simultaneously in separate directories. Useful for reviewing PRs while you keep your work.
- **`git submodule`** vs **`git subtree`** — two ways to embed another repo inside yours. Both are hard; monorepo tools often replace them.
- **`git filter-repo`** — rewrites history to remove sensitive data or huge files. Nuclear option.
- **Signed commits (GPG or SSH)** — cryptographically verify who authored a commit. Enforced in many enterprises.
- **Conventional Commits** (`feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:`) — a standard commit-message format that enables automated changelog generation and semantic versioning.

flashcards.back: Working directory (your edits) → staging area (`git add`) → repository (`git commit`).

flashcards.front: Merge vs rebase — the golden rule?
flashcards.back: Merge combines with a merge commit (safe on shared branches). Rebase replays commits (never on shared branches — rewrites history).

flashcards.front: How to recover a lost commit?
flashcards.back: `git reflog` — it shows every HEAD move; find the SHA, then `git reset --hard <sha>` or `git checkout <sha>`.
Revert appends a new commit that undoes changes (safe on shared history). Reset erases the commit (dangerous on shared history).
Automated binary search through history to find which commit introduced a bug.

options: A movable pointer to a commit
quiz.explain: Just a pointer to a commit.
Rebase creates new commits with new SHAs.

quiz.q: Safest way to undo a commit that's already been pushed to shared main?
quiz.explain: Revert appends an undo commit; safe on shared history.

quiz.q: The safety net for lost work is…
quiz.explain: reflog shows every HEAD position, letting you recover.
src/ is your code — track it. The others are generated or secret.

challenges.text: Init a repo, add a file, commit, branch, edit, commit, merge back. Full round trip.
Create a `.gitignore` for a Flutter project. Include `build/`, `.dart_tool/`, IDE folders, `.env`.
Resolve a merge conflict — create two branches that edit the same line, merge, resolve, commit.
Use `git rebase -i HEAD~5` to squash 5 messy commits into 1-2 meaningful ones.
Recover a 'lost' branch: make some commits, do a hard reset, then use `git reflog` to restore.
Use `git bisect` to find the commit that introduced a bug in a repo you've deliberately broken.
Set up branch protection on a GitHub repo: require PR reviews, passing CI, no force-push to main. Verify by trying to push directly.

What does `git cherry-pick` do, and when would you use it?
What is `.gitignore` and what belongs there?
